layersIdentified: apps/api (api-router: tRPC routers, container.ts DI, apps/api/src/services/* — a second, undocumented 'services' layer living outside packages/application)
apps/web (ui: Next.js App Router, must not import the apps/api runtime container per ADR-063)
apps/ai-worker (worker: not scanned in this pass)
meta.architectureTestEnforcement: partial — with evidence: (1) packages/domain and packages/application boundaries ARE covered by three overlapping/duplicated regex-based vitest suites (tests/architecture/domain-dependencies.test.ts, application-dependencies.test.ts, boundary-tests.ts) PLUS two dependency-cruiser rules (no-domain-to-adapters, no-domain-to-external-infra in .dependency-cruiser.cjs:33-59) — this half of the boundary is genuinely strong. (2) The apps/web -> apps/api runtime-container boundary (ADR-063) is enforced by both a vitest suite (web-worker-boundary.test.ts) and 3 dependency-cruiser rules — also strong, and independently verified clean by this audit (grep for @intelliflow/db|@prisma/client in apps/web/src returned zero non-test hits). (3) There is NO enforcement anywhere — neither dependency-cruiser nor a vitest architecture test — for the packages/application -> packages/adapters boundary via dependency-cruiser (only the weaker regex vitest test covers it), NOR for the apps/api-internal rule implied by ADR-047's own code example ("routers call use cases, not repositories/ctx.prisma directly"). That gap is exactly where the real violations in this report (HEX-001 through HEX-005) live, undetected by CI. (4) tests/architecture/dependency-rules.ts is a tautological test that asserts a hardcoded literal object equals itself — it performs no file/import scanning and provides no real protection despite reading as an architecture enforcement test.
meta.summary: packages/domain is genuinely clean of infrastructure imports (verified by direct grep, not just by trusting the test suite). The domain/application package-level boundary is reasonably well enforced by redundant (if duplicated/drifted) tooling. However, the actual production hot path for two real features — support tickets (TicketService/TicketRoutingService/LeadRoutingService in apps/api/src/services) and appointment scheduling (apps/api/src/modules/legal/appointments.router.ts) — bypasses the hexagonal composition root entirely: a fully-built TicketRepositoryPort + PrismaTicketRepository + InMemoryTicketRepository trio exists in packages/application and packages/adapters but is never instantiated in container.ts; TicketService is instead constructed with a raw PrismaClient. Similarly, appointments.router.ts was partially migrated (5 mutation endpoints now route through use cases per its own 'Phase 2a-2e hexagonal migration' comments) but ~30 read/query/case-link/attendee call sites still hit ctx.prismaWithTenant directly, a state container.ts's own comment (lines 611-614) acknowledges as temporary. A billing-domain port (BillingRepositoryPort / InvoiceRepository / ReceiptRepository) is fully defined at both domain and application layers but has zero adapters and zero consumers — billing.router.ts talks to the Stripe API directly instead. The root structural enabler of all of this is that apps/api/src/context.ts puts raw `prisma` and `prismaWithTenant` on the tRPC Context available to every router, with nothing in CI to stop a router from reaching past `ctx.services`/`ctx.container` straight to the database.

findings.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: `grep -n "TicketRepository" apps/api/src/container.ts` returns 0 matches — container.ts never imports or instantiates PrismaTicketRepository or InMemoryTicketRepository. Yet packages/application/src/ports/repositories/TicketRepositoryPort.ts defines the full port contract (re-exported from packages/application/src/ports/repositories/index.ts:13), and packages/adapters/src/repositories/PrismaTicketRepository.ts + InMemoryTicketRepository.ts implement it. `grep -rl "PrismaTicketRepository" apps/` and `grep -rl "TicketRepositoryPort" apps/` both return 0 files under apps/ (only non-code hits in Sprint_plan CSV/YAML metadata). Instead, container.ts:564 does `const ticketService = new TicketService(prismaClient);` — TicketService (apps/api/src/services/TicketService.ts:19) takes a raw PrismaClient in its constructor.
findings.impact: A complete, presumably-tested hexagonal implementation for tickets (port + 2 adapters) is dead code that never runs in production. The actual runtime behavior is governed entirely by TicketService's ad-hoc Prisma queries, so any bug fix or behavior change made to PrismaTicketRepository/TicketRepositoryPort has zero effect on the live system — a maintainer editing the 'correct' hexagonal layer would ship no change at all.
findings.rootCause: The hexagonal migration for the ticket module appears to have been half-completed: the port/adapter pair was built (likely for an earlier plan) but the router/service wiring was never switched over, and no test asserts the port is actually consumed.
findings.policyViolation: ADR-047-hexagonal-architecture.md (API layer should depend on use cases wired through the container, not construct services against raw infrastructure); ADR-010-architecture-boundary-enforcement.md (KPI: 0 boundary violations, but this class of violation is outside test scope)
findings.remediation: Either (a) wire PrismaTicketRepository into container.ts and refactor TicketService to accept TicketRepositoryPort instead of PrismaClient, or (b) if TicketService's raw-Prisma approach is intentionally kept for now, delete the orphaned port/adapter pair and document the decision in an ADR so the dead code doesn't mislead future contributors.

findings.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: apps/api/src/services/TicketService.ts:1-19 imports `type { PrismaClient, Ticket, TicketStatus, TicketPriority, SLAStatus } from '@intelliflow/db'` and declares `export class TicketService { constructor(private readonly prisma: PrismaClient) {} }`. This class lives under apps/api/src/services/ (API/driving-adapter layer per ADR-047's package structure), not packages/application/src/usecases/, and is coupled directly to Prisma rather than to TicketRepositoryPort (see HEX-001).
findings.impact: Business/orchestration logic for the entire ticketing feature (SLA calculation, status transitions, assignee resolution — see getAssigneeProfiles at TicketService.ts:41) is untestable without a real/mocked Prisma client and cannot be reused by any other driving adapter (e.g. a future worker or CLI) without dragging in apps/api. It also cannot benefit from the InMemoryTicketRepository already built for fast unit tests.
findings.rootCause: apps/api/src/services/ has become a second, parallel 'application layer' outside packages/application, with none of the architecture-test coverage that packages/application/src/ enjoys.
findings.policyViolation: ADR-047-hexagonal-architecture.md package structure (Implementation Notes: application/usecases should own orchestration; adapters/repositories should be the only Prisma-coupled layer)
findings.remediation: Migrate TicketService's orchestration logic into a packages/application/src/usecases/TicketUseCases.ts consuming TicketRepositoryPort, with TicketService (if kept in apps/api) reduced to a thin adapter that just forwards to the use case.

findings.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: `grep -n "class TicketRoutingService" apps/api/src/services/TicketRoutingService.ts` matches; container.ts:567 does `const ticketRoutingService = new TicketRoutingService(prismaClient);` passing the raw PrismaClient (same `prismaClient: PrismaClient` parameter used for `createServices(prismaClient: PrismaClient)` at container.ts:523). No TicketRoutingRepositoryPort exists in packages/application/src/ports/repositories/.
findings.impact: Same class of issue as HEX-002: ticket-routing business rules are directly coupled to Prisma inside the API tier with no port abstraction, so routing logic cannot be unit-tested without a database and cannot be swapped/mocked cleanly.
findings.rootCause: Same pattern as TicketService — services created directly under apps/api/src/services/ during feature development without a corresponding packages/application use case or port.
findings.remediation: Same remediation family as HEX-002: extract to packages/application/src/usecases/ behind a port, or explicitly document apps/api/src/services/ as an accepted secondary application layer in an ADR if the team intends to keep this pattern.

findings.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: container.ts:570 does `const leadRoutingService = new LeadRoutingService(prismaClient);`. `ls apps/api/src/services/` confirms LeadRoutingService.ts is co-located with TicketService.ts and TicketRoutingService.ts under apps/api/src/services/, the same raw-PrismaClient-constructor pattern as HEX-002/HEX-003. Note: this is distinct from the properly-wired `leadRepository = new PrismaLeadRepository(prismaClient)` (container.ts:306) used by LeadService — the lead module has BOTH a correct hexagonal path (LeadService via LeadRepositoryPort) and a bypass path (LeadRoutingService via raw Prisma) coexisting.
findings.impact: Inconsistent architecture within the same bounded context (leads): one code path (LeadService) is properly hexagonal, another (LeadRoutingService, used for IFC-030 lead routing) is not. This makes it harder for contributors to know which pattern to follow when extending lead functionality, and lead-routing rules are untestable without Prisma.
findings.rootCause: Feature-by-feature organic growth: routing logic (IFC-030) was added later than the core LeadService and didn't follow the same DI convention.
findings.policyViolation: ADR-047-hexagonal-architecture.md; internal consistency within the leads bounded context
findings.remediation: Refactor LeadRoutingService to depend on LeadRepositoryPort (already defined and wired) instead of a raw PrismaClient, reusing the existing PrismaLeadRepository adapter.

findings.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: `grep -c "prismaWithTenant" apps/api/src/modules/legal/appointments.router.ts` = 48 occurrences (28 distinct query/mutation call sites) e.g. line 515 `ctx.prismaWithTenant.appointment.findMany(...)`, line 662 `.findMany`, line 681 `.user.findMany`, line 1150-1280 appointmentCase/appointmentAttendee create/delete, line 1296-1337 stats aggregation. Contrast with the SAME file's mutation endpoints which DO route through the container: line 487 `container.scheduleAppointmentUseCase.execute(...)`, line 753 `container.rescheduleAppointmentUseCase.execute(...)`, line 891 `container.completeAppointmentUseCase.execute(...)`, line 923 `container.cancelAppointmentUseCase.execute(...)`, line 1024/1077/1108 `container.checkConflictsUseCase.*`. container.ts:611-614 documents the gap explicitly: 'Router currently talks to AppointmentDomainService + direct Prisma; these use cases are the canonical path for new callers and will replace the direct-Prisma mutations in a follow-up refactor.'
findings.impact: The list/getById/listByCase/stats/case-linking/attendee-management surface of the single largest router in the legal module (1369 lines, apps/api/src/modules/legal/appointments.router.ts) bypasses the application layer entirely for reads and several mutations (case links, attendee add/remove), meaning tenant-scoping, validation, and business rules for those operations live only in router code with no reusable/testable use-case boundary and no repository abstraction. Any future consumer (a worker, a CLI, a different tRPC surface) cannot reuse this logic without duplicating raw Prisma calls.
findings.rootCause: A phased hexagonal migration ('Phase 2a-2e') covered only the 5 mutation endpoints most exposed to conflict-detection risk (schedule/reschedule/cancel/complete/checkConflicts); the read-heavy and case/attendee-linking endpoints were left on the pre-migration direct-Prisma pattern, an explicitly acknowledged and still-open piece of technical debt per container.ts's own comment.
findings.policyViolation: ADR-047-hexagonal-architecture.md (API layer example in the ADR shows routers calling use cases, never ctx.prisma directly); self-documented debt in apps/api/src/container.ts:611-614
findings.remediation: Complete the migration: introduce AppointmentQuery use cases (list/getById/listByCase/stats) and case/attendee-link use cases in packages/application/src/usecases/scheduling/, backed by AppointmentRepositoryPort methods, and remove the remaining ctx.prismaWithTenant.appointment.* calls from the router.

findings.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: packages/application/src/ports/repositories/BillingRepositoryPort.ts:1 is `export type { InvoiceRepository, ReceiptRepository } from '@intelliflow/domain';` re-exporting interfaces defined in packages/domain/src/crm/billing/InvoiceRepository.ts and ReceiptRepository.ts. `grep -rn "implements InvoiceRepository|implements ReceiptRepository" packages/adapters` returns 0 matches — no adapter implements either interface. `grep -rl "InvoiceRepository|ReceiptRepository|BillingRepositoryPort" apps/api` returns 0 files. Instead, apps/api/src/modules/billing/billing.router.ts (lines 206-209) defines its own inline `getInvoice/payInvoice/listInvoices` methods on a Stripe-facing interface and calls the Stripe API directly (comment at line 381-385: 'Currently fetches all invoices from Stripe then slices in memory').
findings.impact: A billing repository port defined at both the domain and application layers is entirely dead — no adapter, no consumer. It signals an intended persistence-backed invoice/receipt model that was abandoned in favor of a live Stripe-API-only approach, which is architecturally fine but leaves misleading dead abstractions in the codebase that could confuse a contributor building new billing features into thinking a port exists to implement against.
findings.rootCause: Early billing design assumed a persisted Invoice/Receipt aggregate; the shipped implementation queries Stripe live instead, and the now-unused port/interfaces were never removed.
findings.policyViolation: ADR-047-hexagonal-architecture.md (dead ports undermine the 'clear contracts through port interfaces' stated benefit)
findings.remediation: Either implement a PrismaInvoiceRepository/PrismaReceiptRepository (if local caching/audit trail is ever needed) or delete InvoiceRepository.ts, ReceiptRepository.ts, and BillingRepositoryPort.ts as unused code, and remove the BillingRepositoryPort export from packages/application/src/ports/repositories/index.ts:17.

findings.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: .dependency-cruiser.cjs `forbidden` array (lines 3-134) contains exactly 6 rules: no-circular (warn), no-orphans (info), no-domain-to-adapters (error, packages/domain only), no-domain-to-external-infra (error, packages/domain only), and 3 apps/web-tier rules (no-web-imports-worker-only-modules, no-web-imports-api-container, no-web-imports-api-context-router). There is no rule with `from: { path: '^packages/application' }` anywhere in the file — run `grep -n "packages/application" .dependency-cruiser.cjs` to confirm zero matches. ADR-010-architecture-boundary-enforcement.md documents dependency-cruiser as an alternative/complement enforcement layer and explicitly lists 'application-dependencies.test.ts' as the mechanism for that boundary — i.e. the application layer relies solely on the weaker regex-based vitest scanner (see HEX-009/HEX-010 for that scanner's own weaknesses), never on the CI-blocking static-analysis tool.
findings.impact: The application layer's dependency boundary (must not import packages/adapters, @prisma/client, etc.) has a single point of enforcement instead of the 'three-layer' defense-in-depth ADR-010 describes for the domain layer. A regression that a regex scanner misses (e.g. via a re-export chain or a new import pattern not yet added to FORBIDDEN_APPLICATION_IMPORTS) has no second check to catch it.
findings.rootCause: dependency-cruiser rules were added incrementally per-incident (ADR-063 web-tier rules were added in response to a production outage); no equivalent incident has yet forced a dependency-cruiser rule for packages/application.
findings.policyViolation: ADR-010-architecture-boundary-enforcement.md ('three-layer enforcement strategy' — architecture tests + ESLint + CI; dependency-cruiser is the more rigorous static-analysis complement per its own 'Alternatives Considered' section, which was adopted for domain and web but not application)
findings.remediation: Add a dependency-cruiser rule mirroring no-domain-to-adapters for `from: '^packages/application'` forbidding `to: '^packages/adapters'` and infra node_modules packages, matching the pattern already used for packages/domain.

findings.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: tests/architecture/dependency-rules.ts:8-59 defines `describe('Hexagonal Architecture Dependency Rules', ...)` with a hardcoded local object `const rules = { domain: { canDependOn: [], cannotDependOn: [...] }, ... }` (lines 9-22) and every subsequent `test()` block only asserts properties of THIS SAME literal object against itself (e.g. line 26 `expect(rules.domain.canDependOn).toEqual([])` — trivially true because `rules.domain.canDependOn` was declared as `[]` two lines above in the same file). The file never calls `fs.readFileSync`, never scans `packages/`, and has no `getTypeScriptFiles`/import-extraction logic anywhere — unlike the other 4 files in tests/architecture/ which do real file scanning. This file is picked up by `pnpm test:architecture` (package.json:37, `vitest run tests/architecture`) and will always report 3 passing tests regardless of the actual codebase state.
findings.impact: A reviewer or CI dashboard seeing 'Hexagonal Architecture Dependency Rules — 3 passed' in the architecture test suite gets false assurance; this file provides zero regression protection despite reading as a real architectural gate. It also duplicates rules already covered by domain-dependencies.test.ts and application-dependencies.test.ts, adding maintenance surface without safety.
findings.rootCause: Likely written as a documentation/specification stub (to make the intended rules explicit in code) and never converted into or replaced by a real scanning test.
findings.policyViolation: ADR-010-architecture-boundary-enforcement.md Layer 1 (Architecture Tests) — intended to be 'comprehensive boundary verification using file scanning', which this file does not do
findings.remediation: Delete tests/architecture/dependency-rules.ts (its intent is already covered by domain-dependencies.test.ts, application-dependencies.test.ts, and adapters-dependencies.test.ts which perform real scans), or convert it into an actual dependency-cruiser-driven check.

findings.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: tests/architecture/boundary-tests.ts:68-143 ('Architecture Boundary Tests' > 'Domain Layer') reimplements the same regex-based import-scanner pattern (getTypeScriptFiles/hasImportsFrom) as tests/architecture/domain-dependencies.test.ts, independently. Compare forbidden-import lists: boundary-tests.ts:119-127 checks only 6 patterns (@prisma/client, @intelliflow/db, prisma, redis, ioredis, pg, mysql), while domain-dependencies.test.ts:26-70 (FORBIDDEN_DOMAIN_IMPORTS) checks 20+ patterns including express, fastify, koa, @trpc/*, hono, react, next, vue, svelte, mongodb, amqplib, kafka, bullmq, @aws-sdk, @azure, @google-cloud, fs-extra. boundary-tests.ts's list has already drifted stale relative to the more complete list — if a domain file imported 'next' or 'bullmq' directly, domain-dependencies.test.ts would catch it but boundary-tests.ts's equivalent 'domain MUST NOT depend on infrastructure' test (line 114) would not.
findings.impact: Two independently-maintained copies of the same enforcement logic create drift risk: a future edit to broaden the forbidden-import list in one file (as already happened) silently leaves the other file weaker, and a contributor reading only boundary-tests.ts would believe the domain layer is protected against a narrower set of violations than it actually is against (both files currently pass, so no live violation, but the redundancy itself is the finding).
findings.rootCause: Both files were likely written independently during different IFC-131 sub-tasks without consolidating into a single shared scanner utility.
findings.policyViolation: DRY / duplicated-interfaces per FINDING-SCHEMA category; not a direct ADR violation but undermines ADR-010's maintainability goal
findings.remediation: Consolidate boundary-tests.ts, domain-dependencies.test.ts, application-dependencies.test.ts, and adapters-dependencies.test.ts into a single parameterized test file (or shared `tests/architecture/lib/scan-imports.ts` helper) with one authoritative FORBIDDEN_IMPORTS table per layer, removing the duplicate scanner implementations.

findings.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: tests/architecture/domain-dependencies.test.ts:163 (`findViolations`) has `if (imp.path.startsWith('.') || imp.path.startsWith('@/')) { continue; }` — relative imports are unconditionally skipped before being checked against FORBIDDEN_DOMAIN_IMPORTS. The identical skip exists in application-dependencies.test.ts:142 and boundary-tests.ts's `hasImportsFrom` implicitly (its regex only matches the captured import string, but a relative path like `../../adapters/src/X` would still be captured and NOT match any of the `@intelliflow/*`-anchored forbidden patterns since those patterns are package-name regexes, not path regexes). No test in tests/architecture/ verifies that a domain or application file cannot reach packages/adapters/src or packages/db via a relative filesystem path instead of the package alias.
findings.impact: Theoretical bypass: nothing currently exploits this (this audit found no relative-path escapes into adapters from domain/application), but the detection mechanism has a structural blind spot. Given pnpm workspaces resolve packages via symlinks in node_modules, a relative path from packages/domain/src into packages/adapters/src on disk would technically resolve at the filesystem level even though it's semantically a cross-package violation.
findings.rootCause: The regex scanners were designed around the package-alias convention (@intelliflow/*) and never hardened against relative-path traversal, since normal development practice doesn't produce such imports.
findings.policyViolation: ADR-010-architecture-boundary-enforcement.md intent (0 boundary violations) — not currently violated, but not verifiably prevented either
findings.remediation: Extend findViolations()/hasImportsFrom() to also resolve relative imports against the file's directory and flag any resolved path that lands under packages/adapters/, packages/db/, or apps/ from a domain/application source file.
findings.falsePositiveJustification: No actual relative-path violation was found in the codebase during this audit — this is a gap in the detection mechanism, not a confirmed live violation. Flagged as a hardening opportunity, not a current breach.

findings.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: apps/api/src/context.ts:97-107 `BaseContext` interface exposes `prisma: PrismaClient` alongside `container: Container`, `services: Services`, `adapters: Adapters`. The `Context` interface (lines 117-128) additionally exposes `prismaWithTenant?: PrismaClient`. Both are populated for every request (createContext, lines 679-728: `prisma: apiPrisma` at line 714). This ambient raw-DB-access property is what structurally enables HEX-005 (appointments.router.ts) and the other ctx.prisma call sites found via `grep -rl "ctx\.prisma\." apps/api/src/modules` (audit.router.ts, inbound.router.ts, conversation.router.ts, plus 3 test files) — 27 total occurrences across 6 files.
findings.impact: Every one of the ~70 router files under apps/api/src/modules/ has an equally-easy path to either `ctx.services.X.someUseCase()` (hexagonal) or `ctx.prisma.table.query()` (bypass) with no structural or lint-level friction distinguishing the two. This makes the pattern in HEX-005 easy to introduce and easy to repeat in new routers.
findings.rootCause: `ctx.prisma`/`ctx.prismaWithTenant` were kept on the context 'for backward compatibility and direct queries' (context.ts:6-9 file header comment) when the services/container pattern was introduced, and never removed once the migration should have made them unnecessary for new code.
findings.policyViolation: ADR-047-hexagonal-architecture.md API-layer example (router constructs/calls a use case, never touches Prisma); no ESLint or architecture-test rule restricts ctx.prisma usage in apps/api/src/modules
findings.remediation: Add an ESLint no-restricted-properties/no-restricted-syntax rule (or a new tests/architecture test) forbidding `ctx.prisma`/`ctx.prismaWithTenant` member access inside apps/api/src/modules/**/*.router.ts files outside an explicit allowlist, forcing new/modified routers through ctx.services or ctx.container.

findings.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: apps/api/src/services/appointment-domain.service.ts:41 declares `export class AppointmentDomainService` described (lines 32-40) as applying 'domain logic to appointment operations' using ConflictDetector, imported from @intelliflow/domain (lines 11-22). appointments.router.ts:14 documents 'INTEGRATION: Uses AppointmentDomainService for domain logic' and imports it at line 23 (`import { AppointmentDomainService } from '../../services';`). Separately, packages/application/src/usecases/scheduling/{ScheduleAppointment.ts,RescheduleAppointment.ts,CheckConflicts.ts} independently reference ConflictDetector/domain conflict-detection logic and are invoked via container.scheduleAppointmentUseCase etc. (see HEX-005 citations). Both AppointmentDomainService (API layer) and the scheduling use cases (application layer) sit between the router and the domain's ConflictDetector for overlapping appointment operations.
findings.impact: Two parallel orchestration layers for the same bounded context (appointment conflict detection / scheduling) increase the risk that a business-rule fix applied to one (e.g. buffer-time handling) is not mirrored in the other, since this audit did not find AppointmentDomainService delegating to the use cases or vice versa — they appear to be siblings, not a caller/callee pair.
findings.rootCause: Same partial-migration state documented in HEX-005: the pre-migration AppointmentDomainService bridge was never removed once packages/application/src/usecases/scheduling/* was introduced for the 5 migrated endpoints.
findings.policyViolation: ADR-047-hexagonal-architecture.md (single canonical application-layer orchestration path expected)
findings.remediation: As part of completing the HEX-005 migration, verify whether AppointmentDomainService's remaining callers (the non-migrated read/case-link endpoints) can be re-pointed at the same use cases, then retire AppointmentDomainService or fold its logic into the use cases directly.
findings.falsePositiveJustification: This audit confirmed both classes exist and are both used within appointments.router.ts, but did not trace every call site to definitively rule out one being a thin wrapper around the other's underlying domain calls (both ultimately use @intelliflow/domain's ConflictDetector, so some convergence is possible at the domain layer).

findings.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: `grep -rn "ctx\.prisma\." apps/api/src/modules` matches non-test source in 3 files: apps/api/src/modules/security/audit.router.ts (5 occurrences), apps/api/src/modules/inbound/inbound.router.ts (9 occurrences), apps/api/src/modules/agent/conversation.router.ts (7 occurrences) — 21 direct `ctx.prisma.*` call sites total outside appointments.router.ts (which uses `ctx.prismaWithTenant`, tracked separately in HEX-005).
findings.impact: Confirms the ctx.prisma bypass pattern (HEX-011) is not confined to appointments — it recurs independently in at least 3 other modules across different bounded contexts (security/audit, inbound webhook intake, AI conversation), suggesting it is treated as a normal/accepted idiom by the team rather than an exception.
findings.rootCause: Same as HEX-011 — ctx.prisma is a convenient, always-available escape hatch with no lint-level discouragement.
findings.remediation: Triage each of the 3 files individually for whether the underlying query belongs in an existing/new application use case; lower priority than HEX-005 since these are smaller, more contained call counts (5-9 each vs. appointments' 28).

findings.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: `grep -rn "from '@prisma" packages/domain/src` = 0 matches. `grep -rln "next/" packages/domain/src` = 0 matches. A broader `grep -rln "@intelliflow/adapters|@intelliflow/db|@prisma/client" packages/domain/src` (run during this audit) returned 0 non-comment matches for actual import statements — the only 'bullmq' hits in packages/domain/src/events/case-events.ts:46,452-457 are string-literal type values (`'temporal' | 'langgraph' | 'bullmq'`), not imports, confirmed by direct file read.
findings.impact: None — this is a positive finding. Despite the weak/duplicated test tooling identified in HEX-008/HEX-009/HEX-010, the domain layer is, as of this audit, actually clean of infrastructure imports. This independently verifies (rather than merely trusts) the domain-purity claim in ADR-047/ADR-010.
findings.remediation: No action required. Recommend keeping this grep as a periodic manual spot-check given the enforcement gaps noted in HEX-007/HEX-010.

findings.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: `grep -rln "@intelliflow/db|@prisma/client" apps/web/src --include=*.ts --include=*.tsx | grep -v __tests__ | grep -v '.test.'` returned 0 files, confirming ADR-063's core constraint (apps/web must not import the DB/container runtime) holds in the current codebase, independent of the test suite that also asserts it (web-worker-boundary.test.ts, boundary-tests.ts's 'Web Tier Boundary' describe block, and 3 dependency-cruiser rules).
findings.impact: None — positive finding. The apps/web <-> apps/api tier boundary (the subject of ADR-063 and a real prior production incident) is currently intact and is the most robustly enforced boundary in the codebase (test + dependency-cruiser double coverage).

findings.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: `ls tests/architecture/*.test.ts` lists exactly 5 test files (adapters-dependencies, application-dependencies, domain-dependencies, tenant-procedure-enforcement, web-worker-boundary) plus boundary-tests.ts/dependency-rules.ts (not *.test.ts, so not auto-picked up by the `include: ['**/*.test.ts', '**/*.spec.ts']` glob in tests/architecture/vitest.config.ts:8 — meaning boundary-tests.ts and dependency-rules.ts may not even run under `pnpm test:architecture` unless imported elsewhere; `grep -rn "vitest.config" tests/architecture/package.json 2>/dev/null` and the include glob confirm only *.test.ts/*.spec.ts files execute). None of the 5 real *.test.ts files scan apps/api/src/modules/**/*.router.ts for direct ctx.prisma/ctx.prismaWithTenant usage or for bypassing ctx.services/ctx.container — tenant-procedure-enforcement.test.ts (the only one that scans apps/api/src/modules) checks exclusively for tenantProcedure middleware usage and absence of a `getTenantId` helper function, not for repository/use-case bypass.
findings.impact: This is the direct governance gap that let HEX-005 (appointments.router.ts), HEX-002/003/004 (services/*.ts raw-Prisma classes), and HEX-013 (audit/inbound/conversation routers) ship and remain undetected by `pnpm test:architecture` and the `architecture` CI job (ADR-010's Layer 3). The enforcement suite protects packages/domain, packages/application's declared dependencies, and the apps/web<->apps/api tier boundary, but has no coverage at all for 'does apps/api actually use its own application layer.'
findings.rootCause: IFC-131 (ADR-010) scoped enforcement to inter-package boundaries; no follow-up task extended it to intra-apps/api layering (router -> service -> adapter, per apps/api/CLAUDE.md's own documented pattern).
findings.policyViolation: apps/api/CLAUDE.md ('Router -> Service -> Adapter: Routers call services, services use ports/adapters') — documented pattern with no automated check
findings.remediation: Add a new tests/architecture/api-router-boundary.test.ts scanning apps/api/src/modules/**/*.router.ts for direct `ctx.prisma`/`ctx.prismaWithTenant` member access (with an explicit, reviewed allowlist for accepted exceptions), following the same pattern as tenant-procedure-enforcement.test.ts. Also verify whether boundary-tests.ts/dependency-rules.ts actually execute under `pnpm test:architecture` given the *.test.ts-only include glob, and rename them to *.test.ts or fold them into the files that do run.

findings.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: .dependency-cruiser.cjs:4-12 sets `name: 'no-circular', severity: 'warn'` (not 'error'), meaning `pnpm exec depcruise` will report but not fail the build on a circular dependency anywhere in the scanned graph. tests/architecture/dependency-rules.ts:49-58 separately asserts (tautologically, per HEX-008) that 'no circular dependencies allowed' between domain/application/adapters, but that assertion checks a hardcoded literal object, not the real dependency graph — so no active mechanism actually blocks a circular dependency from merging.
findings.impact: A circular dependency (e.g. domain <-> application, or two adapters modules) could be introduced and merge to main without any CI failure — only a non-blocking warning in dependency-cruiser output, which is easy to miss if the depcruise step in CI doesn't surface warnings prominently.
findings.rootCause: no-circular was likely set to 'warn' deliberately to avoid blocking on pre-existing cycles at the time the rule was introduced, and was never revisited/promoted to 'error' once the graph was cycle-free.
findings.policyViolation: ADR-047-hexagonal-architecture.md (implicit — hexagonal architecture assumes acyclic layer dependencies)
findings.remediation: Run `pnpm exec depcruise . --config .dependency-cruiser.cjs` and confirm zero current circular dependencies; if clean, promote `no-circular` severity from 'warn' to 'error' to lock in that state going forward.
